@jontwo This is an unavoidable consequence of how rescaling works: by interpolation of values (meaning nearby values are averaged together in some way) (see [this Wikipedia entry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bilinear_interpolation) for a simple example). I would argue that your second result is closest to what you actually want, but another option is to add `order=0` to the `rescale` call. This turns to nearest-neighbor interpolation and does not involve adding nearby values together, so it works:

```python
In [6]: transform.rescale(arr, 2, mode='symmetric', preserve_range=True, order=0)

array([[ nan,  nan,  nan,  nan,   3.,   3.,  nan,  nan],

The method `Storage.getStatistics` reported on the last line of the trace is not itself recursive. It does call into the C version of `pickle.dumps`, which is what line 323 does in version 1.0 of this package (line numbers have since changed, but it still does that same basic thing).

What it's pickling is a user-provided object. So what we're seeing here is a user-provided object that cannot properly be pickled. Given that the cache is a *ram* cache and explicitly documented as using a volatile object to store its data, there's no reason to expect that user provided objects should have to be picklable. 

We only pickle the object to get an estimate of its size for statistics purposes. Perhaps we should catch errors during the pickling process and report `False` as the size value (`False` instead of `None` because it can still be used in arithmetic expressions and people might be summing to get a total size)?

@beckermr: I have two examples of making stamps with blends on this branch.
See [blend.yaml](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/tree/%23654/examples/des/blend.yaml) and [blendset.yaml](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/tree/%23654/examples/des/blendset.yaml).

The first (blend.yaml) is the simpler of the two.  It just draws multiple objects on each stamp, but only one is the primary (centered) object, and we don't try to put the neighbors also in the center of some other postage stamp.  But it does do the thing you wanted where a second output file is made that has the neighbor flux subtracted off.  So simulating the output of a perfect deblender.

The second one (blendset.yaml) is more complicated.  It puts each object in the center of a postage stamp.  So if a blend has 3 objects, there will be 3 corresponding postage stamps, with each object in the center of one.  Also, it outputs both the regular blended images and the perfect deblended images as MEDS files and uses real galaxies and PSFEx psfs.  (The other uses parametric profiles.)  There is a second run that does the same thing but outputs to a Tiled image to make it easier to view quickly in ds9.

The custom code for this is in [blend.py](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/tree/%23654/examples/des/blend.py), so that should give you a start at seeing how custom types work in the GalSim config processing.  

I still need to write up good documentation for it, but these examples were useful, since I found a few bugs and added a couple extra hooks that I wanted here, but hadn't thought to add before.

I came up with a theory, tested it, and now I can say that its at the root of both this and #237.

Our config is unreliable, because it applies the static settings where one would expect to get runtime settings.

### Exhibit A

```
tower-cli config verbose false
tower-cli job_template list --project "Examples" -v
```

### Exhibit B

```
tower-cli config verbose true
tower-cli job_template list --project "Examples"
```

You would expect these to both print the same thing, right? Wrong. Exhibit A will not print the lookups for the related project field. Yet it will print debug messages in another place. This is because some dynamic imports are happening, causing the runtime settings to get squashed when deep in the bowels of the `Related` type code.

The same thing is happening in the examples presented above, and I feel fairly confident in saying that _the related lookup is happening on a different Tower server_ in those given examples.

I think even with the download option, the value tower-cli gets back for result_stdout is "stdout capture is missing". The only way I've been able to get it to consistently retreive the full stdout is to add the format=txt_download query parameter and query the Job Stdout endpoint directly. It works, but it is not as elegant as being able to use the tower-cli monitoring functionality.

```
new_job = get_resource('job').launch(job_template=1791, limit='canary', extra_vars=["tfs_buildid: 281", "tfs_artifact_name: Scripts"])
get_resource('job').wait(new_job['id'])

new_job = get_resource('job').status(new_job['id'], detail=1)

url = "https://towerha.myhost.net/api/v2/jobs/{}/stdout/?format=txt_download".format(new_job['id'])
headers = {'Authorization': 'Basic <password>'}

r = requests.get(url, headers=headers, verify=False)

print(r.text)
print(new_job['status'])
```
This has not yet been implemented for projects or organizations.

I'm using the most current version on webupd8 (0.7.2) under Wayland and have what I think is the same issue (although it may differ).  It seems to me that the behaviour is this:

When I'm running an x-native app (running via xwayland automatically), or if I'm in guake itself, the hotkey is recognized.  But if I'm in a wayland app (or the desktop environment alone) the hotkey is not recognized.

I think the problem is GTK2 vs GTK3 shortcuts and how they're implemented (although I could be off-base here, that's just a rank guess).  In any case, until this issue is fixed -- for anyone who loves guake and wants to keep using it, do this:

@MattiSG Getting the version from legislation.openfisca.fr is a good idea too, I might have thought of that. But querying the API itself for that info strikes me as better in many respects - reliable, documented, reassuring.

When testing complex systems I quite often end up looking for (and often adding) a way to dynamically query the running system for a version identifier for the main component *and* any dependencies that are built separately, often to the granularity of build number or commit name. As a developer I'm usually happy when bug reports contain that info ! And it's a time saver for those occasions where you're not sure your continuous deployment script is actually continuous.

Knowing the country package version is a good start though; as @Anna-Livia remarked IRL when we discussed this, the country package declares its dependencies, so the core version can be known. This is perhaps enough for practical purposes and for the time being.

I'll close this as being addressed by the upcoming version, through X-API-Version, and the workaround; if the need comes up again in development I'll invest some time in a PR for richer version info.

> Thanks for the pointers to the RFCs, but I still don't understand the
> threat. Let's assume that DNS is compromised -- what exactly goes wrong if
> we canonicalize to malicious.com before requesting the security context?
>
> We end up sending the token for malicious.com to either malicious.com or
> good.com (depending on what the person who controls DNS desires).
>
>    - If the token is sent to good.com then authentication fails, so there
>    is no harm done (it's not a new DoS attack because the DNS controlled could
>    always blackhole your request to some bad IP anyway).
>    - If the token is sent to malicious.com then the malicious guy gets
>    your token for malicious.com, but this can't be a problem. (Reasoning:
>    if you control DNS then even if krb does no canonicalisation, you could
>    always have the client generate a token for good.com and have it sent
>    to malicious.com. If *that* isn't a problem, then malicious.com
>    getting a token for their own malicious service cannot be a problem.)
>
> So, again, I don't really see the threat? Like I say - I might just be
> missing something.
>
> I appreciate that the RFCs use the "MUST/SHOULD NOT" language, but
> standards are not always a good guide about how to build a functioning
> member of the web ecosystem, and this case accepted practice for Kerberos
> auth in the web context clearly differs from what these documents say.
>
> Finally, whether we choose to canonicalize or not, we are making *some*
> policy choice which may conflict with the accepted policy at the use site.
> I believe the onus of "hardening" Kerberos should be placed on the 0.1% of
> users who understand the security issues involved and can make an informed
> choice to turn canonicalization off in this lib. I do not think we should
> impose the cost of explicitly configuring requests to behave like a browser
> on the 99.9% of users who reasonably expect that behaviour to be the
> default.

```python

Raising an exception will  work, but requires an external try/except block:
```python
try:
    with db.transaction() as t:
       # do stuff, encounter a failure
       raise Exception
except Exception:
    pass
```
That could be avoided if I could call `t.rollback()`.

But if this breaks because it's reusing the transaction object, it'd be sufficient to document that if you use the context manager, you shouldn't mess with the transaction object.

There's also a minor confusion caused by the Google Cloud Datastore docs.  They imply that all consumers should explicitly rollback their own transactions in case of failure.  From https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/transactions:
```
Note that in order to keep our examples more succinct we sometimes omit the rollback if the transaction fails. In production code it is important to ensure that every transaction is either explicitly committed or rolled back.
```
I guess that's probably false in this case, since the SDK is doing it for me.

I've noticed that AWS has been representing this Serverless Application Model as having already been made available under the Apache license:

- [Introducing the AWS Serverless Application Model](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2016/11/introducing-the-aws-serverless-application-model/):

> As part of this release, the AWS Serverless Application Model is made available under the Apache 2.0 license, enabling others in the ecosystem to adopt and incorporate it into build, deployment, monitoring and management tools with a commercial-friendly license.

- [AWS Serverless Application Model (AWS SAM) Supports Inline Swagger and AWS CloudFormation Intrinsic Functions](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2017/02/aws-serverless-application-model-aws-sam-supports-inline-swagger-and-aws-cloudformation-intrinsic-functions/):

> It is available under Apache 2.0.

- [Using the AWS Serverless Application Model (AWS SAM)](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/deploying-lambda-apps.html#serverless_app):

> The specification is available under Apache 2.0 for AWS partners and customers to adopt and extend within their own toolsets. For details on the specification, see the AWS Serverless Application Model.

These descriptions obscure the fact that the source code of the underlying SAM service implementation is not available under Apache 2.0, only the syntax specification document and some example templates are. Without the underlying transformation code, an Apache-licensed specification document is no more practically useful to AWS customers than the "all rights reserved"-licensed documentation covering the underlying CloudFormation service- it is no easier to "adopt and extend" the SAM than CloudFormation or any other closed-source AWS service.

While I appreciate the bold efforts @sanathkr and others at AWS are making to engage external developers in productive collaboration and discussion through third-party services like GitHub, I'm disappointed this project has been representing itself as an open-source project in AWS announcements and documentation in a way that is rather misleading.

Please, open-source the actual implementation code behind this project and I'll be one of its most enthusiastic supporters and advocates. If this isn't possible, at the very least I hope you update the announcements/documentation referencing this project to clarify that only the specification and examples of the Serverless Application Model are currently open source, and not the actual transform implementation. Looking forward to any response, clarification or updates on this.

```
pip install pyopenssl ndg-httpsclient pyasn1
```

That said, installing the above dependencies is a valid work around.

The `dpkg` command doesn't install dependencies, but will only complain if they are missing. You have to install `python3` for the Debian package to work.

Cool concept! Some thoughts....

There are dangers of "marrying Travis too much" if we wanna switch CI, but if we can manage to have things somewhat closely related to `.travis.yml` then migrating won't be too hard. Obviously, we have already done Travis-specific stuff so it's no biggy adding to that. Doesn't seem like Travis has to do lots for this special deployment feature, anyways.

There's a catch of using a `docs` branch: Docs are supposed to be in special branches already, denoting which release they belong to. So if we choose to build special docs in special branches, then those branches should also somehow automatically be specific to the version string. You would have to use `kolibri.__version__` for this purpose, but avoiding it for development versions and instead just use `develop` for that special purpose.

I think the idea is cool, but it requires us to finalize the git workflow. I would vouch for `master` as the current development branch and have all major releases as a `releases/x.y` branch.. but if someone has other ideas, we might wanna take that discussion somewhere else that this PR :)

The problem I see there is that existing title is now below the content itself, so it doesn't serve as a "continuation" of the breadcrumbs, not even visually. Basically, we are breaking 2 patterns here:
1. User expect that the last level of breadcrumb is the current position.
2. User expect to see (read/hear) the main title at the beginning -  it should be both visually and structurally the first thing to see/read/hear in the main content area.

The problem is that the "expected behaviour" differs for different people. For example, with the Docker client, values specified with `-e` take precedence over those specified in environment files, but lots of Compose users would prefer environment files to take precedence over values specified in the `environment` section of a Compose file.

This is why I think that, as a first step, it'd be preferable to simply provide a utility function for parsing environment files and leave it to the user to construct an `environment` dict to pass into `create_container`. That way docker-py isn't dictating the order of precedence.

Use the recommended dictionary notation:
```python
client.containers.create("ubuntu:latest", volumes={'/sys/bus/usb/devices/1-3:1.0': {'bind': '/sys/bus/usb/devices/1-3:1.0'}})
```

> API should support `--mount` functionality that allows explicit specification of `source` and and `destination` in parameter value.

Ouch.. I just found another issue which related to this.

In glyphsLib 2.2.1 (used by fontmake 1.4), the "Keep Glyphs" instance custom parameter was (used to be) exported with the following UFO lib key:

```
"com.schriftgestaltung.Keep Glyphs"
```

That's how fontmake itself expects it to be when it goes to `subset_otf_from_ufo`.

However, in the latest glyphsLib 2.3.0, the same "Keep Glyphs" custom parameter is now being exported using this mouthful:

 ```
"com.schriftgestaltung.customParameter.InstanceDescriptorAsGSInstance.Keep Glyphs"
```

Therefore fontmake cannot see it... 

The `glyphsLib.builder.custom_params` was completely rewritten by @belluzj to support going in both directions (glyphs -> UFOs, and viceversa):
https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/compare/v2.2.1...master#diff-b0b07578ca1cbb8c00dd528d6b044934

I personally don't like that long name (let alone the space between the words, but that's not the point).
The problem is, prefixing it with `com.schriftgestaltung.*`, suggests that this comes from Glyphs.app _the app_ itself, whereas it really is a private key which is understood and meant to be consumed only by glyphsLib, ie. the python library. So, perhaps something like `com.github.googlei18n.glyphsLib.*` would be more appropriate in this and possibly other cases, I don't know.
It's one thing to use `com.schriftgestaltung.*` when Glyphs.app's built-in UFO exporter uses that prefix for the same kind of data. But if glyphsLib is storing new stuff in the UFO lib for its own use, it should not pretend that those come from Glyphs.app.

Btw, it's not just fontmake that needs to handle the new lib key prefix scheme from glyphsLib 2.3, but also ufo2ft, eg. here:
https://github.com/googlei18n/ufo2ft/blob/dbb107c8093bb8824c7e26fa69d240d3accdb548/Lib/ufo2ft/constants.py#L8

CLAs look good, thanks!

Providing an [AppImage](http://appimage.org/) would have, among others, these advantages:

- One app = one file = super simple for users: just download one AppImage file, [make it executable](http://discourse.appimage.org/t/how-to-make-an-appimage-executable/80), and run
- No unpacking or installation necessary
- No root needed
- No system libraries changed
- Works out of the box, no installation of runtimes needed
- Optional desktop integration with `appimaged`
- Optional binary delta updates, e.g., for continuous builds (only download the binary diff) using AppImageUpdate
- Can optionally GPG2-sign your AppImages (inside the file)
- Works on Live ISOs
- Can use the same AppImages when dual-booting multiple distributions

[Here is an overview](https://github.com/probonopd/AppImageKit/wiki/AppImages) of projects that are already distributing upstream-provided, official AppImages.

Alright, I might be wrong here. The asyncio documentation is not well documented on how to deal with cancelled tasks.

After some googling, I found a [locked issue comment](https://github.com/python/asyncio/issues/253#issuecomment-271529854) that is talking about the exact same issue. That is:

- If you cancel a task, and don't catch the `CancelledError`, it **SHOULD NOT** be awaited
- If you cancel a task, but catch the `CancelledError`, it **SHOULD** be awaited otherwise one can get errors at shutdown (python exiting).

Commit fd4146d fixes the problem where the Manager process can exit before the others //if you send the TERM to the parent process//. It doesn't address the issue where you send a TERM to the Manager process itself (either alone or in combination with the parent, e.g. like systemd does). I've been an on-and-off user of Diamond for quite a few years now. When I first started using it (and the two times I've come back to it since then), I was horribly confused by how the plugins are packaged. While it does facilitate easier distribution of plugins, managing dependencies is unintuitive and it's very un-pythonic.

I'd like to express my 1000% support for this proposal. Keeping compatibility with the current method is great for people who already have deployment/management tooling built around it, but supporting separate plugin packages discovered via entrypoints is definitely what I'd expect from Python software.

For what it's worth, my opinion on "core" collectors vs separate packages is:

* Core collectors should be part of diamond itself, and should have no dependencies that diamond itself doesn't have. i.e. plugins that have a Python dependency that wouldn't otherwise be required by Diamond, they should be moved to a separate package
* Maybe the best way to release this is by incrementally moving plugins to their own packages, and making those setup.py requirements of diamond itself. Let that go out in the wild for a while, where non-core plugins are split into separate packages but required by Diamond. Once that seems stable, do a major version bump and remove the non-core plugins from Diamond's requirements.

Just some thoughts.

I don't really think there are any conflicting interests here. What I suggest hopefully supports different ways of working, ranging from installing a full python environment with your favourite IDE fully integrated, to a very simplistic approach requiring only docker and a text editor. I definitely don't want to impose any specific environment or set of tools on the developer, rather the other way around.  

# Docker
First I will explain the way we use docker in this use case (as it is a versatile tool and can be used in manny ways) to avoid misunderstandings. 
What we do here is to use docker to encapsulate tools. Further more the containers have a very short life cycle of only one operation, for instance running the tests. 
What happens is 

1. A builder container is started with the project files mounted in the container
1. The task is executed in the container, operating on the mounted files
1. The container is terminated and removed

The container is based on a builder image from Bitcraze (bitcraze/builder) so there is no image to build for the user, only pulling it the first time it is used. Starting the container is instantaneous and not noticeable when used on the command line. There is a slight penalty in speed on OSX since the mapping of the filesystem is not that good.
The toolbelt is mainly a tool to make it easy to run these short lived containers.

# Travis
To me travis adds a number of useful things

1. Automatic build on updates to the repository
1. The ability to run tests on multiple versions and environments
1. Publishing build results and status
1. The definition of a successful build

1., 3. and 4. are generic CI/CD server functionality, while 2. is travis specific.

# Questions
I was probably not very clear in my questions, sorry!

What I'm interested in is if .2 of the travis functionality could be implemented in some other way? Maybe we could have a docker image with the tools and versions needed (venv?, tox?, various versions of python, ?) and a script that runs the builder container with the appropriate settings to test the variations we are interested in. This would also enable us to run all tests, for all versions locally.

# Conclusions
I hope I have managed to explain what I'm looking for. I agree that the complexity of the project increases slightly, but only in the automated build part. We will still support a pythonic way of working to cater for the experienced developers that expects a "normal" python environment, and will also enable less python oriented people to contribute.

Trying to distill everything from this thread down into a general strategy for Deis w/ BYOS-- both how it can be accomplished today (with difficulty) and how it could (hopefully) be made easier in the future.

General strategy:

1. Set configs up front (i.e. before install and start).
  * This includes some configs that look to be duplicates and could probably be smoothed over a bit with a little effort.
2. Customize images:
  * Customize `deis-controller` and `deis-logger` to eliminate dependency on `deis-store-volume` OR create a dummy `deis-store-volume` image.
  * Customize `deis-registry` to solve #3429.
  * Customize `deis-builder` to source user keys from database instead of etcd.  I don't believe anyone has done this yet.  See issue #3486.
3. Some unit files need to be customized to eliminate dependencies on store components:
  * `deis-controller`
  * `deis-logger`
  * Dummy `deis-store-volume`?
4. Load only units for _non-store_ components:
  * This means operators need a full awareness of all the Deis components that need to be run and need to install each of them individually.  That may change from one version of the platform to the next-- which makes this knowledge a moving target.
5. Start only units for _non-store_ components-- _in the correct order!_
  * Order matters a great deal here.  It again requires the operator to have detailed and up-to-date knowledge of platform internals-- and that can be a moving target.

I think that's it.  @Blystad, does this look like an accurate summary to you based on your experience?

One more thing worth noting is that for anyone externalizing all platform state, `deis-logger` is also a completely superfluous component.  `deis-logspout` _could_ be configured to send logs _directly_ to wherever it is we'd want to be draining them to from the `deis-logger`.

From my perspective, I feel like all of this is painful enough that a built-in means of accomplishing this feels warranted.  That's purely my own opinion.  I still like the idea of "installation profiles."

@carmstrong, since there was a gut reaction to the notion of "installation profiles" being a potential maintenance burden (which I can well appreciate), I think it's worth saying that if such a feature were to be added, I imagine it being done in a manner that does _not_ create additional maintenance burden.  Above, @Blystad and myself have both mentioned "customized" unit files and images.  The word "customized" may have implied maintaining alternate unit files or images for a "stateless-platform" installation profile, but that wasn't something I'd _intended_ to imply.  What I actually imagine is _not_ creating/maintaining separate images and unit files, but rather remediating some of the issues mentioned above such that all Deis components are more loosely coupled to the storage subsystem.  Alternate installation profiles would thus do nothing more than select and install a particular subset of the official images / unit files.

Thoughts?

I guess the library doesn't stringify because stringifying is dangerous for a few reasons:

1. If the object is cyclic it will throw - yes you can try catch and/or write some logic to detect and remove cycles but it might not yield the result the client is expecting.

2. If the stringified version is too long it will cause the telemetry to be rejected by the server. The client can still pass in a string that is too long and hit the same issue, but I feel if the library is abstracting this, it makes the issue harder to detect and fix.

In my opinion, it is most flexible and consistent to leave it up to the client to figure out how to stringify their own objects.

@ilanschnell Let's consider this urgent please. Thanks!

Basically, in peoples mind, there are 3 types of environments:
- the main python environment
- additional packages environment
- additional binary/lib environment

So each environment can be treated as source for python, source for packages (using site packages environment), source for bin/libs.

Let's assume we have
- EnvA
  - binA
  - PythonA
  - PkgA1
  - PkgA2
- EnvB
  - binB
- EnvC
  - binC
  - PythonC
  - PkgC1
  - PkgC2

So I would suggest extending the activation logic to something like this:
```bash
activate EnvA
activate -p EnvB
activate -b EnvC
```
Would setup something like this:
- Env
  - binC:binA
  - PythonA
  - PkgA1
  - PkgA2

In contrast:
```bash
activate EnvA
activate -b EnvB
activate -p EnvC
```
Would setup something like this:
- Env
  - binB:binA
  - PythonA
  - PkgA1
  - PkgA2
  - PkgC1
  - PkgC2

Or even weird as:
```bash
activate EnvC
activate -b EnvA
activate -b EnvC
```
Would setup something like this (to make sure that the order of search for packages would be preserved as needed):
- Env
  - binC:binA:binC
  - PythonC
  - PkgC1
  - PkgC2

Regarding deactivate - by default it will deactivate the last activate.
There will be also additional switch to make a specific deactivation of some environment.
And additionally a way to deactivate all environments.

This will allow to write strange scripts like this one:
```bash
activate EnvA
   do_only for_A
   activate -b EnvB
      do_for_A_and_B
   deactivate
   activate -p EnvC
      do_for_A_and_C

deactivate -all
do_something_else
``` 
I'm just thinking that without decent definition of atomic operations within activate - there is no way to stack the environments properly.

#!/bin/sh
_CONDA_ROOT="/Users/applemacbook/anaconda"
#!/bin/sh

_conda_set_vars() {
    # set _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR
    if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=bash
    elif [ -n "${ZSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=zsh
    elif [ -n "${KSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=ksh
    elif [ -n "${POSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=posh
    else
        # https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/120138/92065
        local _q="$(ps -p$$ -o cmd="",comm="",fname="" 2>/dev/null | sed
's/^-//' | grep -oE '\w+' | head -n1)"
        if [ "$_q" = dash ]; then
            _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=dash
        else
            (>&2 echo "Unrecognized shell.")
            return 1
        fi
    fi

    #
https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/4650/determining-path-to-sourced-shell-script/
    local script_dir
    case "$_CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR" in
        bash) script_dir="$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")";;
        zsh) script_dir="$(dirname "${(%):-%x}")";;  #
http://stackoverflow.com/a/28336473/2127762
        dash) x=$(lsof -p $$ -Fn0 | tail -1); script_dir="$(dirname
"${x#n}")";;
        *) script_dir="$(cd "$(dirname "$_")" && echo "$PWD")";;
    esac

    if [ -z "${_CONDA_ROOT+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_ROOT="$(dirname "$script_dir")"
    fi

}

_conda_script_is_sourced() {
    # http://stackoverflow.com/a/28776166/2127762
    sourced=0
    if [ -n "${ZSH_EVAL_CONTEXT:+x}" ]; then
      case $ZSH_EVAL_CONTEXT in *:file) sourced=1;; esac
    elif [ -n "${KSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
      [ "$(cd $(dirname -- $0) && pwd -P)/$(basename -- $0)" != "$(cd
$(dirname -- ${.sh.file}) && pwd -P)/$(basename -- ${.sh.file})" ] &&
sourced=1
    elif [ -n "${BASH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
      [ "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" = "$0" ] && sourced=1
    else # All other shells: examine $0 for known shell binary filenames
      # Detects `sh` and `dash`; add additional shell filenames as needed.
      case ${0##*/} in sh|dash) sourced=0;; *) sourced=1;; esac
    fi
    return $sourced
}

if ! _conda_script_is_sourced; then
    (
      >&2 echo "Error: activate must be sourced. Run 'source activate
envname'"
      >&2 echo "instead of 'activate envname'."
    )
    exit 1
fi

_conda_set_vars
. "$_CONDA_ROOT/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" || return $?
_conda_activate "$@"

```

Just jumping in here from ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues#7469 but also because the timestamp issue just came up again for me.  I think the time a package is built is a very valuable piece of information, especially when debugging.

As a user I would like to be able to see or easily find out the time any particular package was built ...which makes the current implementation somewhat problematic because not only is the current timestamp not human-readable but it's got a difficult-to-discover magic factor of 1000 difference from the obvious (to an experienced dev) unix timestamp.

I'm fine which the timestamp value being kept as-is but I think it would be very useful if the output of `conda info <package_name>` did the conversion behind the scenes to output a human readable timestamp.

I think the timestamp info is that useful that I'd also like a `--with-timestamp` argument to `conda list` which would include the converted/human-readable timestamp in the output.

Not high-priority but I'm happy to open separate issues to track them if they're considered reasonable/feasible feature requests.

There is a problem in both. The main problem is that activate.bat does not allow environment variables to get set permanently by gdal-activate.bat.

gdal-activate.bat needs to set %GDAL_DATA% so GDAL can find its data files.

Second, less serious problem is that gdal-activate.bat isn't quite right, should read:
set "GDAL_DATA=%~dp0\..\..\..\Library\share\gdal"
The current syntax ends up with %GDAL_DATA% being quoted which confuses GDAL.

there is a conda package in my channel, I'm using my channel, and I'm still getting this error message.

So, basically `conda 4.4.x` has a problem with "orphan" packages that don't have their specs in `conda-meta/history`.
The first reproducer installs `fontconfig`, a package that depends on `libpng` and `libxml2` which both depends on `zlib` (`>=1.2.11` for the versions being installed).
If `fontconfig` is installed, `libpng` and `libxml2` remain in the environment but don't have any specs about themselves in `history`. If we now try to install a `zlib <1.2.11`, we get
```
UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - libxml2 -> zlib[version='>=1.2.11,<1.3.0a0']
  - zlib[version='<1.2.11']
```
. Interestingly this only happens if we have at least two packages depending on `zlib`. If we remove either of `libpng` or `libxml2`, the packages get downgraded as expected.

Yes, you're right.

It's because snapshot metadata is stored on S3 and this metadata is needed by `GET http://localhost:9200/_snapshot/s3_repository/_all`. If your lifecycle moves these metadata files to Glacier, the problem appears.

To fix it, I just moved all metadata files from Glacier to S3 and forced the lifecycle to just move to Glacier on path `indices`.

It seems to work.

I think I may not be properly explaining the issue.

So, msrestazure version 0.4.0 to 0.4.12 requires keyring. 0.4.13 to 0.4.27 has keyring as optional, and 0.4.28 removes keyring as a dependency. This is a breaking change within the same minor version (which should maintain backwards compatibility). This means that any upstream repository that has specified `msrestazure~=0.4.X` in accordance with [PEP 440](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#compatible-release) has been broken if it relies on keyring being available. 

The azure-batch package is an example of this. Version 4.0.0 specifies msrestazrue in setup.py with: [install_requires "msrestazure~=0.4.11"](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/fbc86e377617b7c20b7876f5cc8198c381d362ce/azure-batch/setup.py#L81). This means that the newest patch update 0.4.28 is pulled in as a dependency. [Since the batch client relies on keyring being installed](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/azure_3.0.0/azure-batch/azure/batch/batch_auth.py), this causes a `ModuleNotFoundError: no module  named 'keyring'` when you try to auth.

To repro:
1. `pip install azure-batch==4.0.0`
2. `python -c 'import azure.batch.batch_auth'`
3. stacktrace:
```
>>> import azure.batch.batch_auth as batch_auth
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\...\lib\site-packages\azure\batch\batch_auth.py", line 9, in <module>
    import keyring
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'keyring'
```

Do you need it for debugging or is it for some documentation or similar? My thesis for my master´s degree did something similar and this was basically just a first version for the example browser. Next up would be some kind of body dimensions evolution together with the neural network weights.

From the top of my head, the following is the approach of this example:
The simulation implements a classic version of evolutionary algorithm[1], which evolves a controller for a fixed morphology using a population of 8 legged individuals. The individuals start with an randomly initialized controller and compete with each other to move away from the starting point as fast as possible. The controller of each individual, which is evolved for walking speed, is a simple one layer neural network that maps from leg ground contact to a certain body joint position. Its weights are evolved either by random weight reinitialization or by crossover of two successful specimen. The population is ranked by walking speed in order to keep the best performing individuals, mutating the less performant ones and cull some of the worst performing ones. The culled individuals are either replaced by new individuals with randomly initialized or crossover-produced controllers. Using this approach, the population as a whole learns to walk and approaches a good local optimum of a walking controller.

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_algorithm

Erwin, [this](https://github.com/RobotLocomotion/drake/issues/1712) is the issue in [Drake](http://drake.mit.edu/) that @sherm1 is referring to. Essentially we are using ray casting for a simple lidar model. The test in [that issue](https://github.com/RobotLocomotion/drake/issues/1712) is simply a 2D lidar placed in front of a box. Some strange returns were observed when they ray was hitting the box close to being perpendicular to it (the zero angle in the plots).
I found out that Bullet resolves the ray collisions with the call to [btSubsimplexConvexCast::calcTimeOfImpact](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/src/BulletCollision/NarrowPhaseCollision/btSubSimplexConvexCast.cpp#L38). In that method there is a hardcoded tolerance to be [epsilon = 0.0001](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/src/BulletCollision/NarrowPhaseCollision/btSubSimplexConvexCast.cpp#L76)) and for some hits the iteration barely converges within that tolerance (for us it translated into a 1 cm error) in 8 iterations. Details are offered in Drake's [PR 2354](https://github.com/RobotLocomotion/drake/pull/2354).
The solution I found to work just two days ago was to change the default algorithm (the sub simplex convex cast) with a GJK convex cast. This is done by setting the option `btTriangleRaycastCallback::kF_UseGjkConvexCastRaytest` for the `ClosestRayResultCallback`. 

We have the following questions:
1. Are these algorithms (GJK and sub sumplex) applicable to concave shapes as well? (their names led us to think that they might only work with convex shapes).
2. Is there a particular advantage (computational cost, robustness) that any of these two algorithms might have?
3. Would it be possible to expose this  [epsilon](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/src/BulletCollision/NarrowPhaseCollision/btSubSimplexConvexCast.cpp#L76) for the user through the API? Ideally I would like to set it to a relative (no units) tolerance. For instance, 1% of some reference length of the object (like the minimum edge length of the object's OBB).

I am working on a very simple demo in my Bullet's fork with just this test (ray vs. one box) in case you are interested in seeing it. I'll post the link soon.
You already have conflicts...

Either 
(1) you use a rigid body with a stick collision shape, and make sure the friction completely puts the object at rest
(2) create a custom action, similar to the btRaycastVehicle or btKinematicCharacterController: perform a ray cast and apply custom forces to move the object. You can still use a rigid body for the body (like the btRaycastVehicle) or not.

There is no easy way to add non-physical one-way interactions integrate this into the regular Bullet constraint solver, aside from using btRigidBody::setLinearFactor and/or btRigidBody::setAngularFactor (which makes the rigid body infinitely heavy in certain directions.

I would suggest working on (2), a custom character controller with or without rigid body torso.

You can use CSS to show/hide each of the different output *types* (result, display, stdout, stderr). Each output type has a different css class, so you can use `display: none` on these classes to hide what you want. The notebook does *not* have semantic information about things like warnings, though, so 'intention' is not available information. That would have to be done in the kernel, e.g. by using Python's warning filter system in the case of IPython.

I don't think you can generally distinguish between *types* of output for what's used for development and what's used for presentation. I have done plenty of presentations were I use every single output type Jupyter understands. You may, at the library level, be able to distinguish between what is 'presentation output' and what is 'debugging output', but Jupyter cannot, and probably most Kernels cannot, either.

Yeah, unfortunately we're caught between two limitations of UIs in the browser. We can't use Ctrl/Cmd in most of our own shortcuts, because almost every Ctrl shortcut is already used by the browser, so we either can't capture them, or people complain when we do. But on the other hand, we can only access the system clipboard when we're handling a clipboard event from the browser - which the user has to trigger with the system clipboard shortcuts.

For now, we've left in the x/c/v shortcuts using our own clipboard-like mechanism that only works within one page, in addition to the ctrl-{x/c/v} shortcuts for using the system clipboard. That's bound to be a source of confusion, but on the other hand we're bound to upset people if we remove the old shortcuts.

@bintoro, looks like a safe and sound solution given the constraints.  

There's are two other solution to this problem that we haven't discussed.  They're a bit obvious, and divergent from the current design, but might as well throw it out there.

1. Place methods in a separate namespace:

  ```python
  m = M._.convert({'convert': True})
  m.convert == m['convert']
  print m._.keys()
  m._.validate()
  m._.to_primitive()
  ```

  Kind of ugly. And of course, the latter will have the limitations associated with a lack of a `keys` method.

2. Place data in a separate namespace:

  ```python
  m = M.convert({'convert': True})
  m.data.convert == m.data['convert'] == m['convert']
  print m.keys()
  m.validate()
  m.to_primitive()
  ```

  The `data` attribute-lookup objects will not have any methods.  For design aesthetics with compound objects, attribute-access will always lead to attribute-lookup objects.  If you want methods, you must go through item access:
  
  ```python
  class N(Model):
      thing = BooleanType()
      subthing = ModelType(M)

  n = N()
  # once you use the `data` namespace, you'll always get attribute-containers:
  n.data.thing = True
  n.data.subthing.convert = True
  print n['subthing'].keys()
  ```

  Otherwise, you'd have to do `n.data.subthing.data.convert` which I think would be terrible.

These are the only ways to guarantee that conflicts won't occur.  

My main concern with all of the solutions discussed so far (beyond their slightly esoteric nature) is that they constrict future growth:  adding a new method to the base `Model` *could* break someone's model.  Even though we're closing in on a reasonable work around, I think that the desire to avoid breaking user's code will be a burden and will hamper development in the future.

Just to be clear, I'm not worried about *you specifically* breaking other projects :) What I'm worried about is someone new who's just starting to learn peru (maybe even just starting to learn bash), and the instructions for their project say

    # Put the following line in ~/.bashrc
    PERU_FILE_NAME=our-special-name

The new developer does what they're told and then a week later, when they try to build someone else's random vim plugin, everything is broken, and they have no idea why.

That wouldn't really totally be our fault, but it's *kind of* our fault, because we encouraged projects to use that global setting. So if we can accomplish the same goals without the risk of confusing people, that would be great.

This is kind of like how Make has the `--makefile` flag, but no environment variable to set it globally. Because it would break so much stuff.

Specifically, updating ``.travis.yml`` to explicitly uninstall the default ``six`` installation and replace it with the required version removes the race condition:

```
install:
  # Pin six to >= 1.11.0 to avoid setuptools/pip race condition
  # For more info, see: https://github.com/OpenKMIP/PyKMIP/issues/435
  - pip uninstall -y six
  - pip install six>=1.11.0
  - pip install tox
  - pip install bandit
  - pip install codecov
  - pip install slugs
  - python setup.py install
```

Another issue I just realized with requiring the charm to clear the flags is how does a charm start using them safely?

I imagine this is how you hope to see them used:
```python
@when('upgrade.series.pre')
def prepare_dist_upgrade():
    host.service_stop('foo')
    apt.purge('foo')
    reactive.clear_flag('upgrade.series.pre')
```

The problem is, what if there had previously been a distro upgrade on this unit? The flag will have already been set and not cleared, because the charm didn't care before. When the new code lands to support upgrades better, the handler will run and maybe harmful things happen because a distro upgrade isn't actually happening; the flags were set by charms.reactive, and clearing them deferred to a charm that didn't care. And when the charm starts caring, it lacks information to handle the flag lifecycle correctly.

You can't resolve the situation n a `@hook('upgrade-charm')` or `@when('hook.upgrade-charm')` handler, as handler does not know what state the system was in when the flag got set, so does not know if it should be cleared. It can either leave the flag set and hope the handlers are idempotent, or clear the flags and risk skipping handlers that should be run. Triggers, however, do provide an option, provided charms.reactive always clears the flag and resets it, causing triggers to fire even if the flag was already set. Which is adding more confusion, because you now have some flags that fire their triggers even when the flag does not change state, which is not the default behavior.

I think this applies to all reactive managed flags to some extent, including the most common like config.changed. It pretty much mandates that handlers using them for guards must be idempotent, or requires using triggers in all cases rather than just in base layers.

```python
register_trigger(when='upgrade.series.pre', set_flag='my.upgrade.series.pre')

@when('my.upgrade.series.pre')
def prepare_dist_upgrade():
    host.service_stop('foo')
    apt.purge('foo')
    reactive.clear_flag('my.upgrade.series.pre')
```

I'm not feeling that this direction change is for the better. I think things are getting worse for charm authors. and it will be harder to write correct charms than it is now. There is more to understand, not less. If we must avoid 'set for the duration of the hook', then I think the problems can be avoided by setting and immediately clearing the flag. Charm authors would be required to use register_trigger, which would do the right thing. I think this is preferable to allowing charm authors to use alternative mechanisms, which are potentially foot guns. Best practice would become 'always use register_trigger', and we end up in the same situation but with more confusing documentation and needing to maintain the behavior for backwards compatibility.

I also personally don't believe that 'set for the duration of the hook' is actually a problem, and that charm authors are quite able to understand and reason about what this means. I personally think the best way forward is to keep the flag lifecycles as they are, where they are cleared automatically at the end of the hook. Documented best practice would be to use register_trigger, because the pattern avoids the various race conditions entirely. We end up with simpler and more correct charms using a single, no matter if reading code in a charm layer or a base layer. And we never have to worry about changing how the charms.reactive flag lifecycles are maintained and backwards compatibility, because it doesn't matter when following best practice.

I think we would be better off just documenting how to use triggers, and maybe coming up with easier ways to use them, making the flag lifecycle changes moot.

The App name is not affected by multidex. It is stored inside  Androidmanifest.xml and not in one of the dexes.
There is always only one manifest, regardless how many dexes are in the apk.
Multidex is just a way oft splitting large Codebasis up, as each der file has limits in how many methods, classes and strings it can store.

Have you compiled the application yourself? Can you share the apk as well as the source code?
I guess that the compiler optimizes the manifest and changes common names to ones from the system resources.

Did you install the cert using only then `cert.pem` file, without `fullchain.pem` or `cert.pem`?  If so, it would have been missing intermediate CA certificates.  Such certificates are "transvalid", and only browsers that had cached the right intermediate CA certs beforhand will trust them.

You can test that with this website:
https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/

And if we did do this as along the lines suggested by @nitmir we'd have to pick between these three paths:

1. Invoke the supplied command with `Popen(..., shell=True, ...)`, which allows people to do semicolon delimitation like `'publish_tlsa; sleep 3600; service nginx reload'` but makes 2 below impossible.
2. Try to check whether the command is in the PATH first, and provide a helpful error in case PATH mangling produced a problem.  But that requires us to know what the command being executed is, which is not sane if it's being parsed by the shell and can contain `;`, `&&`, etc.
3. Don't help the user with PATH problems at runtime, but print the PATH seen by the client in the output for `run_on_successful_renewal`
3. Modify our [invocation of the client](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-auto#L1674) in `letsencrypt-auto` to preserve the entire environment or just PATH.  Preserving the whole environment is easy (sudo has a flag for it); preserving PATH is hard to do safely.  The consequences of letting environment variables in to the client are unknown.  There might be no security problem, but it still makes us slightly nervous.  Also users of packaged variants of the client who call `sudo letsencrypt` rather than `letsencrypt-auto` will have to figure this out for themselves...

@OskarPersson I think your project structure doesn't work well for i18n of sphinx becaues:

- `/path/to/subproject/a/docs`  expects locale files like: `index.po` and `core.po`.
- `/path/to/subproject/a/docs/core/locale` provides `index.po` instead of `core.po`

I think that you can get the expected behavior if you set it like the following:

1. set `gettext_compact = True` to all of `conf.py`.
2. symlink like bellow:
```
$ cd /path/to/subproject/a/docs/locale/sv/LC_MESSAGES/
$ ls
core  index.po
$ rm -R core
$ ln -s /path/to/coreproject/docs/locale/ja/LC_MESSAGES core
```
`subproject` expect `core/*` message catalog files for each `docs/core` files and now there are.

I think this solution is not  a proper way (and no proper way), but it worked in my environment.

Although I agree that common design pattern for validator is to return list of errors, this is not how it is done right now: value of validation function is not used anywhere, you need to raise ValidationError instead. Reporting errors as an exception has some advantages, e.g. you can mix deserialization with validation and stop deserialization when you encounter error you can not proceed with.

Dotted syntax seem to be the case for specifying attributes for "only" and "exclude" schema parameters, I haven't seen mention of plans to use it for error reporting. Moreover, having errors for particular sub-object grouped together is more convenient than filtering strings by prefixes. Compare 
```
{
  'foo.bar.baz': 'Error 1',
  'foo.bar.bam': 'Error 2',
  'foo.quux': 'Error 3',
}
```
to
```
{
  'foo': {
    'bar': {
      'baz': 'Error 1',
      'bam': 'Error 2',
    },
    'quux': 'Error 3',
}
```
If you want to parse errors and display them in place with edit controls, the second variant is far easier to work with.

@thunderfish24 and @adriesse thanks for your comments!

@adriesse I'll address yours first:

>Would it be possible to put in some slow, brute-force functions into PVLIB that we trust will give precisely the right answers? Then all optimized methods can be compared to them for accuracy.

That's the beauty of Bishop's method. It's like having your cake _and_ eating it too because it's ...
* an explicit, "brute force" method that works for single or double diode modes and never BLOWS UP!
* laser fast and internally vectorized, I suppose you could easily add [Numba](https://numba.pydata.org/) or [NumExpr](http://numexpr.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_guide.html) for even further speedups using [llvm](https://llvm.org/) or compiletime optimizations.

But this is only used for calculation `i` and `v` when `ivcurve_pnts > 0`. I am still stuck guessing what the value of `i` will be given any specific constraint on operating condition. Given the output, I usually interpolate to find specific values. I did try to use the canned `fminbound` method which is essentially a bisection or golden section method, but it was much slower, bummer.

However I did put in a very _brutish_ estimate of `voc`. And I also put in a limit on the Newton step, because we know at least that operating conditions will be limited to voltages in between `voc` and zero. I can do a quick check on the convergence rate, to make sure it is always positive, ie: getting closer to the answer. Furthermore we can store the last 2 to 3 newton steps and fit a line or parabola to back search _a la_ [Numerical Recipe's in C, chapter 9.6 and 9.7](http://www.nrbook.com/a/bookcpdf.php). I've implemented this before in a [TMW MATLAB FEX contribution, Newton-Raphson](https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/fileexchange/43097-newton-raphson-solver), there is low overhead and it _does_ improve stability better then just damping.

git annoyances strike again! My recommendation is to:

0. add a new "remote" so you can get the latest version on github. This associates the name "upstream" with the url, but does nothing more: ``git remote add upstream https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python.git`` (I made this step 0 since you might already have this remote)
1. fetch the data from the ``upstream`` remote (this doesn't touch your data or any local changes you've made. it just stores it in a hidden directory): ``git fetch upstream``
2. make a new local branch that's based on upstream/master: ``git branch upstream/master samupdate``
3. checkout the new branch: ``git checkout samupdate``
4. (re)apply your new commits
5. push your new branch to github: ``git push --set-upstream origin samupdate``
6. make a new pull request from the ``samupdate`` branch.

I use steps 1-6 for essentially every pull request.

Step 7 is repeat until it's muscle memory and doesn't feel like a hassle. The git flogging will continue until moral improves.

You're talking about this part, right? https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/special.py#L275-L285
```python
    # scale indices from [-1, 1] to [0, width/height].
    x = (x + 1) / 2 * width_f
    y = (y + 1) / 2 * height_f

    # Clip indices to ensure they are not out of bounds.
    max_x = width_f - 1
    max_y = height_f - 1
    x0 = T.clip(x, 0, max_x)
    x1 = T.clip(x + 1, 0, max_x)
    y0 = T.clip(y, 0, max_y)
    y1 = T.clip(y + 1, 0, max_y)
```

To get all the code together, the problem arises in this part: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/special.py#L311-L323
```python
    # use indices to lookup pixels for all samples
    im_flat = im.reshape((-1, channels))
    Ia = im_flat[idx_a]
    Ib = im_flat[idx_b]
    Ic = im_flat[idx_c]
    Id = im_flat[idx_d]

    # calculate interpolated values
    wa = ((x1_f-x) * (y1_f-y)).dimshuffle(0, 'x')
    wb = ((x1_f-x) * (y-y0_f)).dimshuffle(0, 'x')
    wc = ((x-x0_f) * (y1_f-y)).dimshuffle(0, 'x')
    wd = ((x-x0_f) * (y-y0_f)).dimshuffle(0, 'x')
    output = T.sum([wa*Ia, wb*Ib, wc*Ic, wd*Id], axis=0)
```

If `x` is 1, then `x` will become `width_f`, then both `x0` and `x1` will be set to `width_f - 1`. In the interpolation (second code excerpt), all four weights will be zero because `x1_f - x == 0` and `x - x0_f == 0`, and then the output becomes zero as well.

What we want is to replace coordinates outside the image with an edge pixel.

I think the easiest fix would be to change the mapping in the beginning. Note that we've got the same problem for negative coordinates, they'd result in `x0 == x1 == 0` and give zero weights for the interpolation as well. The following should work:
```python
    # scale indices from [-1, 1] to [0, width/height - 1].
    x = (x + 1) / 2 * (width_f - 1)
    y = (y + 1) / 2 * (height_f - 1)

    # Clip indices to ensure they are not out of bounds.
    x0 = T.clip(x, 0, width_f - 2)
    x1 = x0 + 1
    y0 = T.clip(y, 0, height_f - 2)
    y1 = y0 + 1
```

@skaae, am I missing a reason for scaling x/y to width/height instead of (width-1)/(height-1)? And am I missing a reason for simply setting `x1 = x0+1` after correct clipping of `x0`? This should do what we want for all edges. Or did you actually want the image to be zero-padded?

I now managed to implement the import of UserProfiles, reverting back to the `before_import()` method:

```python
class UserProfileResource(resources.ModelResource):

  def before_import(self, dataset, dry_run, *args, **kwargs):
    user_ids = []

    for data in dataset.dict:
      username = data['username']
      email = data['email']
      building = Building.objects.get(pk=data['building'])
      apartment = data['apartment']

      try:
        user = User.objects.get(username=username, email=email)
      except User.DoesNotExist:
        user = User.objects.create_user(username=username, email=email)
        user.first_name = data['first_name']
        user.last_name = data['last_name']
        user.save()

      try:
        location = Location.objects.get(user=user, building=building, apartment=apartment)
      except Location.DoesNotExist:
        location = Location(user=user, building=building, apartment=apartment)
        location.save()

      user_ids.append(user.id)

    dataset.insert_col(0, user_ids, 'user')
    return super(UserProfileResource, self).before_import(dataset, dry_run, *args, **kwargs)

  class Meta:
    model = UserProfile
    fields = ('user', 'username', 'first_name', 'last_name', 'email', 'building', 'apartment')
    import_id_fields = ('user',)
```

You totally get it. We don’t handle constraints, so we cannot really avoid “insane” targets. If none of the resolvers can do the job, we use the concrete execution (which is the less reliable method) result.

Hmm I see now that your URL is a request.cgi URL and not a query.cgi URL. Indeed the bugzilla API doesn't know how to handle those types or URLs, it expects to be passed a query.cgi or buglist.cgi URL. So you'll need to go to the 'advanced search' web UI and generate a query that fetches the data you want, then pass it to url_to_query and it should work.

For example, this URL will find all bugs that have needinfo requestee=kernel-maint@redhat.com: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/buglist.cgi?f1=requestees.login_name&list_id=8731497&o1=equals&query_format=advanced&v1=kernel-maint%40redhat.com

To anyone who is still looking for mps-youtube to add support for getting your youtube subscriptions feed from your youtube account. As of right now that is basically not possible.

I wanted the feature so I was going to try and implement it, however after looking into the youtube API, they removed that ability a while back. With the current API you would have to retrieve a list of all the channels the user is subscribed to, then get the last few videos from every one of those channels, then sort them chronologically. This would be very slow and taxing, and would probably hit the quota very quickly.

The only other way is to just load the actual youtube sub page, and then parse the html. I may end up doing that for my setup, but I probably won't try to commit it here, as I think that goes against the youtube TOS.

I just wanted to put this here just incase anyone comes across this and wants to implement it. I can save them the trouble of finding out you can't. :/

Hopefully youtube will update the API at some point, but I wouldn't hold your breath because this has been an open issue for years on their API, and they have yet to respond. It would seem they are gimping the API on purpose in order to keep end users using their site and not a 3rd party app.

Currently, the only way to remove a permission is to issue a PUT on the associated endpoint. Using a PATCH will only add members.

This is not the final API and we are currently working on the semantics to use in order to delete principals.

One of the proposal that was done was to keep the endpoint like it is right now, and add new sub-endpoints on which it would be possible to:

- issue PATCH with `{"remove": ["tartenpion"]}` on  e.g. `/records/{id}/permissions/read`;
- issue DELETE on e.g. `/records/{id}/permissions/read/tartenpion`.

Just to update, I got around this issue. My company has a firewall that filters the git port. My git version was up to date. I modified the script from

```
git clone --verbose git://git.launchpad.net/~ubuntu-kernel-test/ubuntu/+source/linux/+git/mainline-crack linux-$LINUX_BRANCH --branch $THE_BRANCH --depth 1
```

to 

```
git clone --verbose https://git.launchpad.net/~ubuntu-kernel-test/ubuntu/+source/linux/+git/mainline-crack linux-v4.4-wily
```

The download was over 1 GiB but all seems fine now.

It is hooked up to a DB. In this case, it is using [Neo4j](https://neo4j.com). So, inside `data/player/types.py` is the following:

```python
import graphene

from data.base_abstracts import BaseAbstract

class SchoolAbstract(BaseAbstract):  # BaseAbstract just defines some fields that should be on ALL types
    name = graphene.String()
    slug = graphene.String()
    abbreviation = graphene.String()
    ...

class School(SchoolAbstract, graphene.ObjectType):
    players = fields.NodeList('data.player.types.Player')
    ...

    ###########################
    #        RESOLVERS        #
    ###########################
    def resolve_players(self, args, context, info):
        Player = import_module('data.player.types.Player')
        return RelatedNodeResolver(self, 'Player', args, context, info).execute(Player)
```

Basically, my `types.py` file has two things (perhaps I could break them out into separate files, I have not found the need yet).

1. Abstract classes
1. Object classes

The `Abstract` classes define the fields. The `Object` classes define the resolvers, and inherit from the `Abstract`. This separation makes it so that everything is easy (for me) to find.

As for `RelatedNodeResolver`, I have a set of custom resolvers that I have abstracted away so that each of my resolvers inside the `types.py` files are very short. They put together my cypher queries for Neo4j and execute to return the results. If you are also using Neo4j and curious what that looks like, let me know.

If you don't have any constraints regarding the tools you need to use, would you mind trying Salt? You'd be the very first person outside CloudFlare trying it (I think).

A couple of months ago, we, at CloudFlare, have evaluated the available tools in order to automatise our network. Indeed Ansible is the most used, but didn't cover our expectations. Thus we have picked up Salt from a couple of reasons:

- engineers from other teams already familiar with this environment
- Salt already manages thousands of our servers around the world, thus extremely scalable
- with Ansible we were able only to retrieve and push configs. What happens between those two events, was a huge question mark
- Salt, on the other hand already had all the advantages you would need (covering more than you'd need on the server side):
   - retrieve data/config (module)
   - caching (mine)
   - group devices by properties (e.g. on network side: you can select by vendor, select by firmware, by firmware version, by country, or devices whose hostnames respect a regex, etc, -- actually imagination is the only limit) -- this is called grains
   - config check & enforcement (state)
   - push config (module)
   - save the retrieved data in a specific format, in a specific place (returners)
   - not just retrieve data, but there are also so called "runners" that allow you to go through all of your network devices (or selected group of devices using the grains) and adapt the output to your needs (e.g. we already have in production tools able to find an IP/MAC address and within 2s looks in all our devices, in all MAC Address Tables, ARP Tables and Interfaces IPs)

It already has all the tools you can need: connection to SQL/NoSQL databases (Postgres, MySQL, Cassandra, Redis etc), file management, git, hadoop etc. The list is too long and does not make sense to write down here. I bet you won't have time as much as you can do!

Salt was mainly designed for the server side, but recently they have introduces the Proxy minions. You can use the proxies, having a good library like NAPALM, to connect to the network device and instruct Salt to do all the things you would need.

Over the last months we begun integrating NAPALM in Salt:

- The proxy: https://github.com/saltstack/salt/blob/develop/salt/proxy/napalm.py
- Two modules:
   - NET: https://github.com/saltstack/salt/blob/develop/salt/modules/napalm_network.py
   - NTP: https://github.com/saltstack/salt/blob/develop/salt/modules/napalm_ntp.py
- NTP state: https://github.com/saltstack/salt/blob/develop/salt/states/netntp.py

They will be fully integrated in the next couple of months and there will be much more modules available soon enough.

You can try it out following the instructions I wrote here: https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm-salt

When will be fully integrated in Salt, you'll only need to type two simple command lines and boom: you can manage your network:

```bash
# apt-get install salt-master
# pip install napalm
```

Hi @omerarshad,

The fastText supervised model works as follow: each word (and word ngram) is associated to a vector representation (a.k.a. embedding, in dimension 100 by default). A representation for the input text is obtained by averaging the embeddings corresponding to the words and ngrams that appear in the input. Then, a linear classifier is used on this representation to obtain the score corresponding to each label. When training the model, both the word/ngram embeddings and the linear classifiers are learned, in one step (you are correct that there is two weight matrices, which are learned jointly).

It is also possible to learn word representations on large amount of unsupervised data, using either skipgram or cbow, and then use these representations to initialize the supervised model. When using a small amount of supervised data, this can improve the performance significantly. Please note that we distribute such high-quality pre-trained models on our website ().

Regarding the sentence "This architecture is similar to the cbow model of Mikolov et al. (2013), where the middle word is replaced by a label" from our paper, it means the following. In the supervised model, we predict a label given the full input (without removing any word from the input), while in cbow, we predict the middle word, given the rest of the window. Both models use the same architecture (but data is used differently).

I hope this answers your questions regarding the model.

Best,
Edouard.

Regarding the 15% testing size and the practice of passing it around as a single numpy array, what happens if the dataset is much larger than memory?  This implementation will break in that case, correct?  What is the right way to do this?

> It also looks like it wasn't able to achieve the same level of eventual performance. Thoughts on why this would be? It just isn't able to test as many hyperparameters?

Correct, this is because random sampling is limited to how many hyperparameters it can evaluate. Successive halving evaluates more hyperparameters, but only spends time on ones with higher scores.

> How hard do you think it would be to do this same analysis, but with the machinery in #288?

Trivial – this analysis already uses the machinery in #288! I judged it to be most appropriate to put the graph here because I haven't shown a graph for successive halving or Hyperband yet.

>  Hyperband would presumably end up at the same place as the best of the successive halving calls, but would be pushed far out to the right due to the increased number of successive halving calls. Correct?

Correct. It will wind up with the maximum score over all successive halving brackets, and will be pushed out to the right.

It should certainly be possible, but I'm not sure offhand how much work
it'll be.

You need to tell pybind11 about the `Dog : Animal` inheritance, by changing:

```C++
py::class_<Dog, PyDog, std::shared_ptr<Dog>>(m, "Dog")
```
to:
```C++
py::class_<Dog, PyDog, Animal, std::shared_ptr<Dog>>(m, "Dog")
```
(the specific position doesn't matter, as long as the base class is somewhere in the template argument list).

PR #805 can help with a class that doesn't support placement new: it can handle a pointer, so you could write:

```C++
cl.def(py::init([](Arg a, int b) { return new Class(a, b); }))
```

We could, in theory, automatically map a `py::init<Arg, int>()` to that when the class doesn't support placement new initialization, but it might also be simpler to just document the above as the approach when a class doesn't support placement new.

Re: caching internals, it's just one check:

```cpp
if (internals_ptr)
    return *internals_ptr;
```

The rest of the code where it's used is likely to have more overhead than a single is-null check :) However if you'd like so I'll revert those parts (or add an overload of `get_type_info()`) that takes `internals` as argument as well -- I'm convinced it'd be nice if library code didn't touch `registered_types_cpp` directly if humanly possible).

Re: direct conversions being stored as a pointer in the typeinfo and the actual converters in a separate map - is because this way it doesn't care if `PYBIND11_NUMPY_DTYPE` or `py::class_<>` was called first, since the converters are guaranteed to be auto-magically shared between the two. The only price you pay, really, is a single pointer dereference when it comes to direct conversions. The difference between this case and e.g. implicit conversions is that in this case no error will be thrown if the order of declarations was wrong (i.e. typeinfo didn't exist when the numpy dtype was bound) -- it will just silently imply that direct converters for this dtype won't work -- in other cases I believe you will get an error which is easier to deal with.

You can't directly assign a python callable to a std::function, but you can work around it pretty easily in a couple of ways.

If you don't strictly need an std::function, you can simply change `callback` to a `pybind11::object`—which is callable with the same `()` operator as the std::function.

If you really need an std::function (i.e. because you need to interface with external code that uses one), you could use `def_property` with a py::object variable capture like this:

```C++
.def_property("callback",
        [](Test &t) { return t.callback; },
        [](Test &t, py::object callable) { t.callback = [callable](int param) { callable(param); }; })
```

That should do it. One issue was types derived in Python as mentioned above. The other issue had to do with types which are defined without a scope, e.g. like [`py:: make_iterator`](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/pybind11.h#L1451). The patch above fixes unscoped types by leaking the reference so they end up living forever. The only alternative I can think of is to ban unscoped types.
If it becomes part of 2.1 or 2.2, you can of course #ifdef based on ``PYBIND11_VERSION_MAJOR`` and ``PYBIND11_VERSION_MINOR``

In most cases, you have to explicitly tell Certbot which plugin to use. The common case where this isn't true is when running `certbot` or `certbot run` (they're the same thing) when the Apache plugin is installed (and no [third party plugins](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#third-party-plugins) that are both [authenticators](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#authenticators) and [installers](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#installer) are installed). This is the case when running `certbot-auto`.

In this scenario, the Apache plugin is the only plugin installed that's able to complete the requested task so it is automatically chosen. We've regretted this automatic selection more than once, but we can't change it now. Instead, we should print a message about the plugin being automatically selected.

For the developer who fixes this, we should probably just send a message to `logger.info`.

Hi, I'm sorry that you are experiencing issues with Certbot. Reading the provided log file, it seems that there are two different errors involved. The other that should be easy to fix probably has resulted from either moving part of the Certbot configuration from another machine, or manually deleting a certificate lineage:

```
2018-07-24 00:47:07,817:WARNING:certbot.renewal:expected /etc/letsencrypt/live/................/cert.pem to be a symlink
2018-07-24 00:47:07,817:WARNING:certbot.renewal:Renewal configuration file /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/.................conf is broken. Skipping.
```

This can be easily fixed by removing the said renewal configuration file from `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/` directory.

Another thing that caught my eye was a line:
```
2018-07-24 00:47:01,981:DEBUG:certbot.plugins.util:Can't find apache2ctl, attempting PATH mitigation by adding /usr/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/local/sbin
```

While Certbot is trying to fix the issue for you, there seems to be an issue with cron job / systemd timer you are running,. It has an incomplete `PATH` environment variable value available for it. This exact line isn't the root cause though, as Apache plugin is doing only verification that an installation exists as Apache plugin seems to be used only as an installer, and standalone plugin is used as an authenticator. In this scenario, Certbot should not be shutting down, restarting or starting up your Apache server, but instead expects the port 443 when using TLS-SNI and port 80 when using HTTP validation to be free.

We use `apache2ctl` script for handling the restarts for Apache in Certbot internally. Depending on the distribution, this calls `systemctl` or other distribution specific methods within the used init system to handle the actual restarts. While this is usually interoperable, we have seen some inconsistencies along the way. My suggestion would be to set up the `PATH` variable correctly in the cron job you are using, and substituting the pre- and post hook commands with `apache2ctl stop` and `apache2ctl start` and see how it goes.

Sorry for the rambling wall of text of an answer. To recap suggestions to try to fix the issue:
- Remove the broken renewal file, or fix it if it's still relevant.
- Set up `PATH` env variable in your cron job script, and substitute `systemctl` start/stop commands with `apache2ctl`

Hi @billionaire,

This is a known bug in Certbot where an error message is displayed incorrectly. @ynasser is in the process of fixing this bug, so a future version of Certbot will give a more useful error message in this case.

This error message normally occurs when you're trying to get a certificate using the `--webroot` method, but when the certificate authority connects to your web server to verify that you control the domain name, it receives an error (such as a 404 not found error). The bug in Certbot results when the error message from your web server contains a non-ASCII character; then Certbot is unable to correctly show you exactly what your server said instead of the expected text.

This generally happens because of a mis-specified webroot path (with `-w`) or an incorrectly configured web server. The directory that you specify with `-w` should be the directory where you would put content in order for it to appear on the top level of your web site. For example, if you were trying to get a certificate for www.example.com and you specified `-w /var/www/html`, that means you're saying that a file created at `/var/www/html/example.txt` will be visible to the public on the web at the URL http://www.example.com/example.txt.

Here's a quick hack that I'm using right now.  It borrows heavily from Model.setObjective().

I say it's a quick hack because it doesn't include any kind of error checking, which would be important if this were pushed into the repo version.

```
def chgReoptObjective(self, coeffs, sense = 'minimize'):
        """Establish the objective function as a linear expression.

        :param coeffs: the coefficients
        :param sense: the objective sense (Default value = 'minimize')

        """

        cdef SCIP_OBJSENSE objsense

        if sense == "minimize":
            objsense = SCIP_OBJSENSE_MINIMIZE
        elif sense == "maximize":
            objsense = SCIP_OBJSENSE_MAXIMIZE
        else:
            raise Warning("unrecognized optimization sense: %s" % sense)
        
        assert isinstance(coeffs, Expr)
